brands.name: The Grounds Guys
brands.notes: Landscaping and grounds-maintenance franchise in the Neighborly portfolio (founded 1987 as Sunshine Grounds Care; franchised since 2010). 228 franchised, zero company-owned units in the US/Canada. Mobile, contract-driven model serving residential and commercial accounts. Franchisor markets territories nationwide but publishes no public state-by-state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Wide revenue spread between mature and young franchises
riskFlags.detail: 2024 Item 19: franchises operating 3+ years averaged $854,816 gross sales (median $568,201), but franchises under 3 years averaged just $283,746 (median $235,252) - the median mature unit is roughly 2.4x the median young unit, so new-owner ramp is slow.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Chatter 2025 FDD review (The Grounds Guys)

riskFlags.title: Population-priced franchise fee plus escalating minimum royalties
riskFlags.detail: The FDD prices the initial franchise fee at $35,000 for territories up to 100,000 population, plus $350 per additional 1,000 population - a larger territory raises the entry cost. The 5-6% royalty carries minimum-fee floors regardless of actual sales.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Chatter 2025 FDD review; SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis

riskFlags.title: Stacked ongoing fees on top of royalty
riskFlags.detail: Beyond the 5-6% royalty, franchisees owe a 2% marketing/advertising fund contribution plus local marketing-group contributions of up to 3% of gross sales - combined ongoing marketing-and-royalty burden can approach ~11% of revenue.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; Franchise Chatter 2025 review

totalInvestmentMin.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter review)

totalInvestmentMax.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter review)

franchiseFee.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)
franchiseFee.note: For a territory up to 100,000 population; +$350 per additional 1,000 population.

royaltyPercent.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter review)
royaltyPercent.note: 5-6% of gross sales with minimum fees; excludes 'roll-in' sales.

adFundPercent.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter review)
adFundPercent.note: 2% marketing fund plus local marketing groups up to 3% of gross sales.

avgUnitRevenue.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)
avgUnitRevenue.note: 2024 average gross sales for franchises operating 3+ years; median $568,201. Franchises under 3 years averaged $283,746.

totalUnits.source: The Grounds Guys 2025 FDD outlet tables (via Franchise Chatter review)

yearFounded.source: Neighborly / The Grounds Guys brand history (founded as Sunshine Grounds Care)

yearFranchising.source: The Grounds Guys brand history (joined Neighborly and began franchising)

veteranDiscount.source: The Grounds Guys franchise site; VeteransFranchise.com
veteranDiscount.note: 20% off the minimum initial franchise fee for qualified veterans.

financingOffered.note: No direct franchisor financing; SBA-approved with third-party financing relationships.

businessModelType.source: The Grounds Guys franchisor site (residential and commercial grounds care)

mobileBased.source: The Grounds Guys franchisor site (mobile, route-based service; no retail build-out)

internationalAvailability.source: Neighborly / The Grounds Guys (US and Canada locations)

isGrowing.source: Franchise Chatter 2025 FDD review (228 franchised, up from prior years)

brands.name: NaturaLawn of America
brands.notes: Organic-based lawn-care franchise (founded 1987; franchising since 1989; HQ Frederick, MD), the pioneer of the natural/organic lawn-care category. ~89 franchised + ~10 company-owned units across 20+ states serving 90,000+ customers. Mobile, route-based model. Franchisor publishes no public state-by-state availability list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: High 9% royalty (well above lawn-care peers)
riskFlags.detail: Franchisees pay a 9% royalty on gross sales (reducing to 7% only on renewal) plus a 1% advertising contribution - a combined ~10% ongoing burden that is high for the lawn-care category, where many peers charge 5-6%.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; Vetted Biz

riskFlags.title: Franchise litigation history (terminated/disputing franchisees)
riskFlags.detail: The franchisor has litigated against franchisees, including NaturaLawn of America, Inc. v. West Group, LLC (D. Md. 2006/2007, 484 F.Supp.2d 392) and NaturaLawn of America, Inc. v. Gro-Bro, Inc. (D. Md. 2011) - disputes touching franchise-agreement enforcement and, in the West Group matter, New Jersey pesticide-advertising-law concerns.
riskFlags.source: vLex / Justia court dockets

riskFlags.title: Small, slow-growing system in a regulated product category
riskFlags.detail: With ~89 franchised units across 20+ states after 35+ years of franchising, NaturaLawn is a niche organic-lawn-care brand; pesticide/fertilizer application is state-regulated and requires licensing, adding compliance overhead franchisees must manage locally.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz NaturaLawn of America Franchise Insights; SHARPSHEETS

liquidCapitalMin.source: Vetted Biz / Franchise Direct NaturaLawn summaries

netWorthMin.source: Vetted Biz / Franchise Direct NaturaLawn summaries
netWorthMin.note: Plus access to a line of credit of $75,000-$125,000.

totalInvestmentMin.source: NaturaLawn FDD Item 7 (via Vetted Biz and SHARPSHEETS)

totalInvestmentMax.source: NaturaLawn FDD Item 7 (via Vetted Biz and SHARPSHEETS)

royaltyPercent.note: 9% of gross sales, reducing to 7% on renewal.

avgUnitRevenue.source: Vetted Biz NaturaLawn of America Franchise Insights (FDD-derived)
avgUnitRevenue.note: Estimated yearly gross revenue (AUV) per Vetted Biz analysis.

totalUnits.source: Vetted Biz NaturaLawn of America Franchise Insights

renewalTermYears.note: 5-year initial term; three additional 5-year renewal terms available.

businessModelType.source: NaturaLawn franchisor site (residential organic lawn care)

isGrowing.source: Vetted Biz (system roughly flat at ~89-99 units over many years)

fddAvailable.source: The FDD Exchange; Vetted Biz; SHARPSHEETS

brands.name: U.S. Lawns
brands.notes: Commercial-only landscaping and grounds-maintenance franchise (founded 1986 in Orlando, FL; franchising since 1987), part of the BrightView/Russell Landscape ecosystem of brands. ~210 franchised locations across 37 states. Contract-driven B2B model with owner-operator, semi-absentee, and conversion entry paths. Franchisor markets nationally but publishes no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Tiered royalty that starts at 6% of gross billings
riskFlags.detail: Royalty starts at 6% of gross billings and steps down toward 4% as monthly revenue grows; combined ongoing fees are cited at roughly 8% of revenue - a meaningful drag on a contract-margin B2B landscaping model where revenue is lumpy and tied to renewable commercial contracts.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; FranchiseGrade

riskFlags.title: Revenue concentration risk in commercial contracts
riskFlags.detail: As a B2B franchise dependent on commercial grounds-maintenance contracts (HOAs, office parks, retail), franchisee revenue can swing on the loss or non-renewal of a few large accounts; the model rewards sales/account-management skill more than a retail-traffic concept.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS U.S. Lawns FDD analysis; franchisor site (B2B contract model)

riskFlags.title: Higher-end build cost despite a B2B model
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 7 runs roughly $71,500-$200,000, with the high end driven by trucks, mowing/landscaping equipment, and working capital; conversions of existing landscapers come in lower because equipment is already owned - new entrants face the full equipment outlay.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; FranchisePayback

totalInvestmentMin.source: U.S. Lawns 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and FranchisePayback)
totalInvestmentMin.note: Low end reflects conversions with existing equipment.

totalInvestmentMax.source: U.S. Lawns 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and FranchisePayback)

royaltyPercent.note: Starts at 6% of gross billings, decreasing toward 4% as monthly revenue grows; total ongoing fees ~8%.

avgUnitRevenue.source: SHARPSHEETS U.S. Lawns FDD analysis (Item 19-derived AUV)
avgUnitRevenue.note: Average yearly revenue per franchised business.

totalUnits.note: ~210 franchised locations across 37 states; all franchised.

businessModelType.source: U.S. Lawns franchisor site (commercial-only grounds maintenance)

mobileBased.source: U.S. Lawns model (route/contract-based mobile service; no retail location)

isGrowing.source: SHARPSHEETS / directory data (system roughly flat around 210-250 units)

item19Available.source: SHARPSHEETS U.S. Lawns FDD analysis (AUV disclosed)

brands.name: ComForCare Home Care
brands.notes: Non-medical in-home senior care franchise (first location 1996, Michigan) owned by Best Life Brands (acquired 2017). ~248 franchised, zero company-owned territories in the US. Recurring-revenue caregiving model in a growing aging-demographics category; no retail build-out. Franchisor markets territories nationwide with no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Labor-law / overtime exposure inherent to the caregiver model
riskFlags.detail: Home-care franchisees employ hourly and salaried caregivers and are exposed to FLSA overtime and wage-and-hour litigation; a Pennsylvania ComForCare franchisee (Elder Resource Management) was ordered by a court (DOL action) to pay ~$2.4M in overtime back wages, illustrating the category's payroll-compliance risk.
riskFlags.source: U.S. Department of Labor news release; OvertimePayLaws.org

riskFlags.title: Stacked marketing fees on top of royalty
riskFlags.detail: Beyond the 5% royalty (with weekly minimums), franchisees contribute 1% of gross sales to general marketing, 1% to national advertising, and must spend 2% on local marketing - combined ongoing fees can approach ~9% of gross sales.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; Franchise Direct

riskFlags.title: Reimbursement and caregiver-supply dependence
riskFlags.detail: Revenue depends heavily on private-pay and VA/long-term-care reimbursement, and the business is constrained by chronic home-care labor shortages; recruiting and retaining caregivers is the binding operational constraint on growth for most franchisees.
riskFlags.source: ComForCare franchising materials; home-care industry analysis

totalInvestmentMin.source: ComForCare 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)

totalInvestmentMax.source: ComForCare 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)

franchiseFee.source: ComForCare 2025 FDD (via Franchise Direct and SHARPSHEETS)
franchiseFee.note: Standard $59,000; reduced offering of $29,500 in certain cases.

royaltyPercent.source: ComForCare 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)
royaltyPercent.note: 5% of gross sales with required weekly minimums.

adFundPercent.note: 1% general marketing + 1% national advertising; plus required 2% local marketing spend.

avgUnitRevenue.source: ComForCare 2025 FDD Item 19 (brand-reported; via Franchise Chatter)
avgUnitRevenue.note: 2024 average gross sales across 202 territories open the full year with 13+ months of data.

totalUnits.source: ComForCare 2025 FDD outlet tables (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Chatter)

yearFounded.source: ComForCare franchise story (first location in Michigan)

yearFranchising.source: Vetted Biz / Franchise Direct ComForCare summaries

mobileBased.source: ComForCare model (in-home, caregiver-dispatch; office plus field caregivers)

isGrowing.source: SHARPSHEETS / Franchise Chatter (system at ~248 territories, growing senior-care category)

brands.name: Assisting Hands Home Care
brands.notes: Non-medical in-home senior care franchise (earliest locations ~2002), ~202 franchised + ~5 company-owned units. Recurring-revenue caregiving model with strong Item 19 averages but a very wide spread between top and bottom territories. Franchisor markets territories nationwide with no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Extreme revenue dispersion across territories
riskFlags.detail: 2024 Item 19: 84 franchisees operating 12+ months produced an average of ~$2,149,198 gross revenue, but the range ran from $77,278 (Clinton Township, MI) to $11,209,774 (Cincinnati, OH) - a 145x spread, so the average is heavily pulled by a few large operations and is not a reliable expectation for a new owner.
riskFlags.source: Assisting Hands 2025 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)

riskFlags.title: Caregiver labor-law and wage-and-hour exposure
riskFlags.detail: Like all non-medical home-care franchises, Assisting Hands franchisees directly employ hourly caregivers and carry FLSA/overtime and state wage-and-hour compliance risk; the business is also constrained by persistent caregiver labor shortages that cap revenue growth.
riskFlags.source: Home-care industry analysis; Assisting Hands franchise materials

riskFlags.title: Tiered royalty with escalating weekly minimums
riskFlags.detail: Royalty is tiered (5% below $48,000 weekly revenue, 4.5% to $95,999, 4% above $96,000) with minimum royalties that escalate from $50/week (year 1) to $100/week (year 2) to $200/week thereafter, plus a national advertising fund contribution - fixed obligations that bite hardest on slow-ramping new offices.
riskFlags.source: Assisting Hands 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

liquidCapitalMin.source: Assisting Hands FDD (via FranNet / Vetted Biz summaries)

netWorthMin.source: Assisting Hands FDD (via FranNet / Vetted Biz summaries)

totalInvestmentMin.source: Assisting Hands 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

totalInvestmentMax.source: Assisting Hands 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

royaltyPercent.note: Tiered: 5% below $48,000 weekly revenue, 4.5% to $95,999, 4% above $96,000; escalating weekly minimums.

adFundPercent.note: Greater of 0.5% of gross revenue or a minimum National Advertising Fund contribution.

avgUnitRevenue.source: Assisting Hands 2025 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)
avgUnitRevenue.note: 2024 average gross revenue across 84 franchisees operating 12+ months; range $77,278-$11,209,774. Wide dispersion - treat as a ceiling, not an expectation.

brands.name: Massage Heights
brands.notes: Membership-based massage and wellness spa franchise (founded 2004, San Antonio, TX), ~115-120 locations in the US and Canada. IMPORTANT: the brand rebranded to 'Heights Wellness Retreat' in October 2024 (adding lymphatic drainage, red light, salt therapy, meditation) - it still trades on the Massage Heights heritage. Membership-recurring-revenue model. Franchisor publishes no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: $3.3M jury verdict in a client sexual-assault case
riskFlags.detail: A jury found Massage Heights liable in a client sexual-assault case and awarded $3.3 million - a category-specific liability exposure (therapist misconduct claims) that franchised spa owners inherit, with attendant insurance, screening, and reputational costs.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Times

riskFlags.title: Developer litigation over support and sales-goal defaults
riskFlags.detail: Six Area/Regional Developers sued Massage Heights corporate after receiving default letters, contending sales goals were missed because the franchisor failed to provide adequate support; separately, Massage Heights Franchising LLC v. Hagman (Tex. Ct. App. 2023) reflects ongoing franchisor-franchisee litigation in the system.
riskFlags.source: FindLaw (Massage Heights Franchising LLC v. Hagman); FranchiseComplaints.org

riskFlags.title: High build cost plus 8% combined ongoing fees and a rebrand transition
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 7 runs roughly $445,000-$1,060,000 with a 6% royalty plus 2% ad fund (8% combined); the October 2024 rebrand to Heights Wellness Retreat adds new service lines and conversion costs, and the long-term economics of the expanded model are not yet proven across the system.
riskFlags.source: Franchise Chatter 2024/2025 FDD reviews; SHARPSHEETS

totalInvestmentMin.source: Massage Heights / Heights Wellness Retreat 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Chatter)
totalInvestmentMin.note: Some 2024 sources cite a tighter $472,000-$554,000 range; SHARPSHEETS cites $445,000-$1,060,000.

totalInvestmentMax.source: Massage Heights / Heights Wellness Retreat 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS)

franchiseFee.source: Massage Heights 2024/2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

royaltyPercent.source: Massage Heights 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Chatter)

adFundPercent.note: 2% advertising fund; combined 8% ongoing fees with royalty.

avgUnitRevenue.note: ~$1.03M average annual sales per 2024 FDD-derived analysis.

totalUnits.note: ~115-120 locations across the US and Canada.

yearFounded.source: Franchise Times; SHARPSHEETS (founded in San Antonio, TX)

businessModelType.source: Massage Heights / Heights Wellness Retreat franchisor site (membership spa)

customerAcquisitionModel.value: Membership-based recurring revenue (monthly wellness memberships) plus walk-in/retail services

isGrowing.source: Franchise Times (system around 115-120 units; closures and rebrand transition)

fddAvailable.source: Franchise Chatter 2024/2025 FDD reviews; The FDD Exchange

brands.name: HealthSource Chiropractic
brands.notes: Chiropractic and progressive-rehab clinic franchise (founded 1994; franchising since 2006; HQ Lakewood, OH), ~133 franchised clinics. Build-out-heavy clinic model. Note: a franchisee must typically be or employ a licensed chiropractor, and clinic revenue depends on insurance billing. Franchisor markets nationally with no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Modest unit revenue against a $400K+ build cost
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 19 reported ~$513,184 average gross revenue across ~113 reporting franchise outlets - a modest figure relative to the $421,291-$618,387 initial investment, implying a long payback if a clinic does not exceed the average.
riskFlags.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

riskFlags.title: History of franchisor-franchisee and trademark litigation
riskFlags.detail: The franchisor has been involved in multiple disputes, including Dudley v. HealthSource Chiropractic (W.D.N.Y., Lanham Act/trademark), Dugger et al v. HealthSource Chiropractic (N.D. Ohio 2018, contract), and HealthSource Chiropractic, LLC v. Health Source KC Inc. (D. Kan. 2024, trademark enforcement) - a recurring litigation footprint around the brand and its franchisees.
riskFlags.source: Justia / FindLaw / vLex court dockets

riskFlags.title: Licensed-professional and insurance-billing dependence
riskFlags.detail: Clinic economics depend on a licensed chiropractor (owner or employed) and on third-party insurance reimbursement plus the franchisor's HSWorx billing software; reimbursement-rate pressure and provider recruiting are structural constraints on the model.
riskFlags.source: HealthSource franchise FAQs; Franchise Chatter 2025 review

totalInvestmentMin.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)
totalInvestmentMin.note: Earlier (2024) FDD-derived sources cite a wider $310,000-$534,000 range; 2025 figures higher.

totalInvestmentMax.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)

franchiseFee.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)

royaltyPercent.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)

adFundPercent.note: 2% of gross revenues toward national marketing/advertising.

avgUnitRevenue.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)
avgUnitRevenue.note: Average gross revenue across ~113 reporting franchise outlets (HSWorx billing data).

totalUnits.source: HealthSource Chiropractic 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Franchise Direct)
totalUnits.note: Franchise clinics; Franchise Times Top 400 ranks the brand at #376 for 2025.

yearFounded.source: SHARPSHEETS / Franchise Direct (founded; HQ Lakewood, OH)

veteranDiscount.note: Veteran/military programs advertised; franchise-fee discount amount not clearly published.

businessModelType.source: HealthSource Chiropractic franchisor site (chiropractic + progressive rehab clinics)

customerAcquisitionModel.value: Insurance-billed clinical care plus cash/wellness plans; new-patient marketing offers

brands.name: The DRIPBaR
brands.notes: IV-vitamin-therapy and wellness franchise (founded 2016, Warwick, RI; franchising since late 2019) in fast expansion. RN-administered drips under a national medical director and medical board, LegitScript-certified. The defining due-diligence issue is the lightly-regulated, medically-sensitive nature of retail IV therapy. Franchisor markets nationally with no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Operating in a lightly-regulated, medically-sensitive category
riskFlags.detail: Retail IV therapy is under-regulated and medically sensitive; a high-profile Texas case in which a woman died after IV therapy led the Texas Medical Board to cite a lack of protocols and supervision and restrict the medical director's license - illustrating the patient-safety, medical-director, and liability exposure franchisees in this category carry.
riskFlags.source: Arnolt Center for Investigative Journalism (IV therapy regulation reporting)

riskFlags.title: Medical-director and licensed-clinician dependence (corporate-practice-of-medicine rules)
riskFlags.detail: Each location requires RN drip specialists and a supervising medical director, and many states' corporate-practice-of-medicine and IV-therapy rules govern clinic ownership and supervision - adding clinical-staffing cost, recruiting difficulty, and state-by-state compliance complexity beyond a typical retail franchise.
riskFlags.source: Medical Director Co. / Marti Law Group (IV therapy medical-director requirements)

riskFlags.title: Young system with most units still ramping
riskFlags.detail: Founded 2016 and franchising only since late 2019, most of the system is young; FDD Item 19 reports gross sales grouped by number of months open, indicating a cohort still maturing - long-run unit economics rest on a small base of seasoned locations.
riskFlags.source: The DRIPBaR FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter reviews); SHARPSHEETS

totalInvestmentMin.source: The DRIPBaR 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and FranchisePayback)

totalInvestmentMax.source: The DRIPBaR 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and FranchisePayback)

franchiseFee.source: The DRIPBaR 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Vetted Biz)

royaltyPercent.source: The DRIPBaR 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and FranchisePayback)

avgUnitRevenue.source: The DRIPBaR FDD Item 19 (via franchise analyses)
avgUnitRevenue.note: Reported average annual revenue per franchise; FDD groups gross sales by months open (young cohort).

yearFounded.source: SHARPSHEETS / Vetted Biz (founded in Warwick, RI)

multiUnitAvailable.source: The DRIPBaR 2025 FDD (multi-unit development agreement for 2-10 locations, $192,125-$660,200)

businessModelType.source: The DRIPBaR franchisor site (retail IV vitamin therapy/wellness)

fddAvailable.source: The FDD Exchange (2025 FDD); Franchise Chatter reviews

brands.name: iCRYO
brands.notes: Cryotherapy, IV infusion, and recovery-services wellness franchise (founded October 2015, League City, TX by Kyle Jones; franchising since 2017). Build-out-heavy studio model. The defining due-diligence issue is a very large gap between franchises awarded (280+) and studios actually open (~25-37) - most signed deals have not yet opened. No public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Huge gap between franchises awarded and locations open
riskFlags.detail: iCRYO reports 280+ franchise locations awarded but only ~25 US locations open (22 franchised, 3 corporate) per Vetted Biz, with ~200 in development - a low awarded-to-open conversion rate. A pipeline this far ahead of openings raises questions about how many awarded units will actually open and operate.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz iCRYO Franchise Insights; Fitt Insider

riskFlags.title: Franchisor regulatory and franchisee litigation history
riskFlags.detail: iCRYO Franchise Systems was the subject of a Washington State securities consent order (S-19-2814-20-CO01) tied to its franchise-registration record, and a franchisee (GTM Holdings LLC) filed a contract/debt-collection suit against the franchisor in Texas (later dismissed) - a regulatory and litigation footprint for a young system.
riskFlags.source: Washington DFI consent order; UniCourt (GTM Holdings v. iCRYO)

riskFlags.title: High build cost in a lightly-regulated IV/cryo category
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 7 runs $475,000-$1,205,000 for a service mix (cryotherapy, IV infusion, recovery) that includes medically-adjacent IV therapy - which in many states requires clinical oversight and carries the same under-regulation/patient-safety scrutiny affecting the broader retail IV-therapy space.
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; Vetted Biz

totalInvestmentMin.source: iCRYO 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and Vetted Biz)

totalInvestmentMax.source: iCRYO 2025 FDD Item 7 (via SHARPSHEETS and Vetted Biz)

royaltyPercent.source: iCRYO 2025 FDD (via SHARPSHEETS and Vetted Biz)

totalUnits.note: ~25 US open (22 franchised + 3 corporate); 280+ awarded, ~200 in development. Open count is the operating reality vs. the larger pipeline.

yearFounded.source: Fitt Insider / CIO Bulletin (founded Oct 2015, League City, TX)

brands.name: Perspire Sauna Studio
brands.notes: Infrared-sauna and red-light-therapy membership studio franchise (concept since 2010), ranked #1 sauna franchise by Entrepreneur in 2023-2024. ~44 studios open (38 franchised + 6 company/affiliate) with ~200 territories sold. Membership-recurring-revenue model. Like other emerging studio brands, there is a large gap between territories sold and studios open. No public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Large gap between territories sold and studios open
riskFlags.detail: The franchisor cites ~200 territories sold against ~44-55 studios open - most awarded territories have not yet opened. FDD outlet tables show franchised units growing 17 -> 26 -> 44 across 2021-2023 (with one temporary closure in 2023), so the open base is small and young relative to the sold pipeline.
riskFlags.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD outlet tables (via Franchise Chatter review); SHARPSHEETS

riskFlags.title: High build cost plus 9% combined ongoing fees
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 7 runs $565,538-$989,638 with a 7% royalty plus a 2% national marketing fund (9% combined) - a meaningful fixed-and-variable burden on a membership model whose AUV averaged roughly $546K-$552K for franchised studios in the most recent Item 19.
riskFlags.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

riskFlags.title: Membership-dependent recurring-revenue model with churn risk
riskFlags.detail: Revenue rests on monthly sauna/wellness memberships; like all membership fitness/wellness concepts, profitability depends on member acquisition cost and churn, and a studio that cannot sustain its membership base will underperform the system averages quickly.
riskFlags.source: Perspire Sauna Studio franchisor materials; SHARPSHEETS analysis

totalInvestmentMin.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

totalInvestmentMax.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

franchiseFee.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

adFundPercent.note: 2% national marketing fund; 9% combined ongoing fees.

avgUnitRevenue.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review)
avgUnitRevenue.note: Average unit volume for 25 franchised studios open in 2023; median $552,520. All-studio (30) average was $551,741.

totalUnits.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD outlet tables (via Franchise Chatter); franchisor (~55 open)
totalUnits.note: ~44 open at end of 2023 (38 franchised + 6 company/affiliate); franchisor cites ~55 open and ~200 territories sold.

yearFounded.source: Perspire Sauna Studio franchisor site (concept since 2010)

businessModelType.source: Perspire Sauna Studio franchisor site (membership infrared-sauna studio)

multiUnitAvailable.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD (Area Development Agreement, min $70,000 for two additional locations)

customerAcquisitionModel.value: Monthly membership recurring revenue (infrared sauna + red light therapy) plus single-session sales

isGrowing.source: Perspire Sauna Studio 2025 FDD outlet tables (franchised units 17 -> 26 -> 44 across 2021-2023)

brands.name: Stretch Zone
brands.notes: Practitioner-assisted stretching franchise (founded 2004; franchising since 2017), one of the fastest-growing wellness studio brands - 377 franchised, zero company-owned units, reportedly no closures to date. Membership-recurring-revenue model with a relatively low build cost. Franchisor publishes no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Royalty stepping up from 6% to 7% mid-term
riskFlags.detail: The 2025 FDD sets royalty at 6% of gross revenues, but for gross revenues accruing on or after January 1, 2025 through the balance of the initial term, it rises to 7% - an increase existing franchisees absorb, plus a 2% marketing fee (9% combined ongoing fees).
riskFlags.source: Stretch Zone 2025 FDD (via Franchise Chatter review)

riskFlags.title: Modest median revenue against rapid system expansion
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 19 reports a median annual gross revenue of ~$308,000 across 293 units operating 12+ months (down from ~$392K-$417K medians cited in prior years), even as the system scaled past 377 units - rapid growth has not lifted, and may be diluting, typical unit revenue.
riskFlags.source: Stretch Zone 2025 FDD Item 19 (via Franchise Chatter review); prior-year FDD reviews

riskFlags.title: Litigation/bankruptcy disclosed in FDD
riskFlags.detail: Stretch Zone discloses lawsuits and/or bankruptcy information in its FDD (Items 3/4) per FDD-tracking services - prospects should review the current FDD's litigation section directly for specifics.
riskFlags.source: Vetted Biz / VetMyFranchise Stretch Zone FDD analyses

totalInvestmentMin.source: Stretch Zone 2025 FDD Item 7 (via Franchise Chatter and SHARPSHEETS)

royaltyPercent.note: 6% historically; rises to 7% of gross revenues for revenue accruing on/after 1/1/2025 through the balance of the initial term.

fddAvailable.source: Franchise Chatter 2025 FDD review; Vetted Biz

brands.name: StretchMed
brands.notes: One-on-one assisted-stretching studio franchise (founded 2019, MA; franchising since 2020) using PNF and static stretching in 25- and 50-minute sessions. Small, young system (~31 franchised studios). Membership-recurring-revenue model with a relatively low build cost. Franchisor publishes no public state list, so state availability is omitted.

riskFlags.title: Very young, small system - limited operating track record
riskFlags.detail: Founded 2019 and franchising only since 2020 with ~31 studios, StretchMed is an early-stage system; long-run unit economics rest on a thin base of seasoned locations, and the brand competes directly with far larger assisted-stretch franchisors (Stretch Zone ~377 units, StretchLab).
riskFlags.source: SHARPSHEETS 2025 FDD analysis; Vetted Biz

riskFlags.title: Crowded assisted-stretching category with larger competitors
riskFlags.detail: Assisted stretching has rapidly commoditized, with StretchLab (Xponential) and Stretch Zone holding far larger footprints and marketing budgets; a sub-50-unit brand faces real competitive and brand-awareness disadvantages in shared markets.
riskFlags.source: Assett Franchise analysis (StretchMed: built to last?); SHARPSHEETS best-stretching-franchises

riskFlags.title: Membership-dependent model with thin reported profit
riskFlags.detail: 2025 FDD Item 19 indicates an average annual profit of roughly $110,000 per studio; as a membership concept, profitability hinges on member acquisition cost and churn, and the modest profit figure leaves limited cushion if a studio underperforms on memberships.
riskFlags.source: StretchMed 2025 FDD Item 19 (via 1851 Franchise and Vetted Biz)
